project.title: ZooMS and Shotgun Proteomics for Species Identification of the Nile Softshell Turtle (Trionyx triunguis)
project.projectDescription: We applied nanoscale liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (nanoLC-MS/MS) in a single Trionyx triunguis reference specimen to identify diagnostic COl1 peptide markers. Our analysis revealed four COL1 biomarkers that differentiate T. triunguis from all sea turtle species, as well as seven additional turtle species with COL1 sequences available on NCBI: Chelonoidis abingdonii, Chrysemys picta bellii, Gopherus evgoodei, Mauremys reevesii, Pelodiscus sinensis, Terrapene carolina triunguis, and Trachemys scripta elegans. These findings enhance biomolecular identification methods for freshwater and marine turtle remains in archaeological and ecological contexts (especially on the Levant).
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: To extract proteins from the bone samples we followed a procedure similar to that outlined in. In short, 10mg of bone was demineralised in 1.2M HCl and placed on a rotating rack at 4°C until demineralisation was complete. Then the proteins were extracted using a GuHCL extraction buffer, quantified using a BCA assay and digested by adding Trypsin. Finally peptides were de-salted by ZIPtipping. Samples for ZooMS analysis were spotted out onto a 384-spot MALDI plate and analysed by MALDI-TOF spectrometry at Cambridge University Bioarchaeology facilities. Additionally, we obtained shotgun proteomic data from the 6 museum reference specimens. These were analysed by nanoflow liquid chromatography on an EASY-nLC 1200 coupled to a Q-Exactive HF-X mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific).
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Spectra were mapped against COL1 sequences available on NCBI for nine turtle species: Chelonia mydas, Dermochelys coriacea, Chelonoidis abingdonii, Chrysemys picta bellii, Gopherus evgoodei, Mauremys reevesii, Pelodiscus sinensis, Terrapene carolina triunguis, and Trachemys scripta elegans, using the pFind software. Amino acid substitutions indicated by this step were verified using MaxQuant version 2.0.3.0. We ran both specific and unspecific searches, with the maximum length of peptides in unspecific searches restricted to 40 amino acids. Peptides with good coverage of Y and B ions, especially at differing amino acid positions, were considered reliable.  MALDI spectra were observed in the software mMass, using established processing criteria. In particular, we examined collagen peaks that could distinguish T. triunguis from sea turtle species, as these species can be sympatric on the Levant. We identified four COL1 biomarkers unique to T. triunguis and validated them by cross-referencing LC-MS/MS peptide mass differences observed in MaxQuant with MALDI spectra from the reference specimen.

references.referenceLine: de Kock W, van der Meer MTJ, van Bommel R, Taurozzi AJ, Von Tersch M, Allentoft ME, Mackie M, Ramsøe M, Collins M, Alexander M, Palsbøll PJ, Çakırlar C, Turkozan O. Stable isotope evidence of anthropocene disruption in African softshell turtle foraging. PLoS One. 2026 21(2):e0339589

Characteristics[NumberOfSamples]: Eleven modern and four ancient T. triunguis were sampled in this study.
Characteristics[Specimen]: We examined one ancient T. triunguis bone fragment from the Early Bronze Age settlement Tell Fadous-Kfarabida, situated in the central Levant (Lebanon).
Comment[Instrument]: EASY-nLC 1200 system coupled to an Exploris 480 mass spectrometer (Thermo Scientific)
Comment[Separation]: nanoflow liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS)
Comment[SpecilizedMS]: matrix assisted laser desorption ionisation-time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF)
